channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Middle East. Visa fee. Trump booed. Primaries. Alcohol guidance. Food safety quiz. Birth order. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Trump formally nominates Todd Blanche as attorney general, setting up an uncertain confirmation battle in the Senate
item.description: As acting attorney general, Blanche has steered the typically independent Justice Department toward the president's demands, sometimes drawing bipartisan criticism from lawmakers.

item.title: ChatGPT-maker OpenAI files to go public, is expected to seek valuation near $1 trillion
item.description: The artificial intelligence company would be the third trillion-dollar stock market debut this year, after Elon Musk’s SpaceX and AI rival Anthropic.

item.title: ‘I know firsthand why Graham Platner shouldn’t be a U.S. senator,’ writes Genevieve McDonald, Platner’s former political director
item.description: "I quit the campaign in October, disturbed by what I learned about the candidate and concerned about his potential impact on the Democratic Party’s prospects in my home state," McDonald writes.

item.title: Love coffee? Have the last cup at this time for a better night’s sleep.
item.description: Sorry, that late-day latte may be causing you to lose more sleep than you think.

item.title: Trump’s $100,000 fee on H-1B visas for highly skilled workers is struck down in court
item.description: A judge declared President Donald Trump’s fee unlawful. The fee narrowed a major pathway for legal immigration that is used by tech companies, hospitals and universities.

item.title: After lawmakers object to Mormons being separated from other Christian denominations, Pentagon revises religious categories
item.description: Last week, the Defense Department released a dramatically pared-down list of religious groups for service members, with some faiths classified with the tag “Christian,” but not the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The new list released Monday simply says the names of religious groups, without any broader Christian designation.

item.title: As El Niño develops, this city is seeing beach weather — during winter
item.description: May and June typically mark the onset of cooler temperatures and increasingly chilly weather in Lima, Peru. But things have been different this year. Instead of cooling off, it’s getting warmer.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Family video brings back old flight response to dying mom
item.description: Carolyn Hax: Family video brings back old flight response to dying mom

item.title: Kennedy Center removes Trump's name from its website as it complies with a judge's ruling
item.description: The change came more than a week after a federal judge ruled the center’s board of trustees had illegally renamed the venue and ordered the restoration of its title, without President Donald Trump's name. The front of the venue still reads: “The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.”

item.title: This agent sent models to meet Jeffrey Epstein. Now he’s trying to explain why.
item.description: A Post examination reveals how convicted sex offender Epstein cultivated a network of modeling industry associates who offered to bring women into his orbit after he was released from jail.

item.title: Trump struggles to end an Iran war he never should have started
item.description: All the options are bad, and the president has no one but himself to blame.

item.title: The best and worst moments from the Tony Awards
item.description: “Death of a Salesman” won best revival of a play and “Schmigadoon!” claimed the coveted prize for best musical, but there was one letdown during the night.

item.title: A reality star in today’s On the Record
item.description: A reality star in today’s On the Record

item.title: Iran says it will halt attacks on Israel after waves of strikes
item.description: Israel and Iran traded fresh volleys of long-range missile strikes after a significant weekend escalation that resulted in the first direct blows between the Middle Eastern archenemies since April.

item.title: Iran-Israel strikes. Epstein agent. Trump walkout. World Cup. El Niño. Coffee timing. Sea stars. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Israel launches strikes on Iran hours after Tehran fired a barrage of missiles
item.description: The exchange of fire threatened to further complicate efforts to broker a lasting peace deal to end the months-long U.S.-Israeli war with Tehran and raised the prospect of a return to open conflict.

item.title: 3 red flags that suggest a friendship isn’t worth saving, according to therapists
item.description: It’s never an easy decision, but sometimes you just need to let go. Here’s how to know whether it’s time.

item.title: Iran launches missiles at Israel for the first time since a ceasefire started in April, the Israeli military says
item.description: The apparent attack, which followed Israeli military strikes on Beirut’s southern suburbs earlier Sunday, threatened to further complicate efforts to broker a peace deal aimed at ending the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

item.title: This couple grows trees in the shape of chairs. They sell for $90,000.
item.description: Gavin Munro and his wife are realizing his lifelong dream, taking about 10 years and painstaking effort to guide each chair from the ground up. Some of the chairs are usable as furniture, but for now they are sold as works of art.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Too frozen by fear to confront the end of a marriage
item.description: A letter writer knows the relationship has been irredeemable for years but wonders, “How do people summon the courage?”

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now.
item.description: Play The Washington Post's daily crossword puzzles online for free.

item.title: Why most politicians are not calling for data center bans despite voters’ anger
item.description: Many Americans are furious about the energy-guzzling behemoths that drive artificial intelligence, but politicians in both parties are cautious about backing all-out prohibition.

item.title: After decades of pelvic pain and 100-plus doctor visits, one question changed it all
item.description: Jabbing pain and constant malaise followed him for years, but regular blood tests always came back normal — until one day a specialist thought to ask something new.

item.title: Trump says Reflecting Pool shows his plan for D.C., disturbing critics
item.description: Historians, members of Congress and several federal judges have criticized the president’s “move fast and break things” approach to remaking Washington.

item.title: Here’s the cockroach of credit myths that just refuses to die, our columnist writes
item.description: Some credit card holders still think you need to carry a balance and pay interest on it to build or maintain an excellent credit score.

item.title: Ibuprofen vs. acetaminophen: Which pain reliever should you use?
item.description: Here are some tips for choosing an over-the-counter medication for headaches, body aches, fever and more.

item.title: Top Trump artificial intelligence adviser to leave the White House
item.description: Sriram Krishnan, a tech investor who shaped the Trump administration’s pro-industry AI policies, is expected to start an outside institution that will influence technology policy.

item.title: A Tetris-like house helped two sisters stay connected as they aged
item.description: To age in place, and live near each other, Sandra and Franca Di Diomete decided to build an interlocking home on Sandra’s tiny, odd-shaped lot.

item.title: Three fathers killed their families this week as domestic violence deaths remain high
item.description: The homicide rate is falling, but domestic killings are not. For those who track this kind of violence, the deaths are both predictable and preventable.

item.title: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.
item.description: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.

item.title: Family angry Hegseth, Trump Jr. ‘ghosted’ them on Navy SEAL’s death investigation
item.description: Cmdr. Job Price’s death was ruled a suicide. But irregularities raised suspicions among his loved ones and others.

item.title: CIA officer accused of stealing gold created fake ‘black box’ spy program, according to people familiar with case
item.description: David J. Rush worked on highly secretive intelligence programs and is alleged to have funneled millions of dollars for personal use via a fraudulent contract.

item.title: Her hit divorce memoir wasn’t entirely truthful. Maybe it doesn’t matter.
item.description: Questions have arisen about Belle Burden’s best-selling work and its suggestions about her finances, but female readers are defending it anyway.

item.title: Major oil companies skip Trump’s auction of leases in Arctic National Wildlife Refuge
item.description: The sale of 72,000 acres in leases is nonetheless a step toward drilling in the wilderness home to caribou and polar bears.

item.title: Democratic socialist leads by double digits in D.C. mayoral race, Post-Schar School poll finds
item.description: Janeese Lewis George, a democratic socialist who represents Ward 4 on the D.C. Council, has been boosted by voters who find her more honest and think she will do a better job handling issues of affordability and public schools. She leads Kenyan R. McDuffie, a former at-large council member, by 11 points in the Democratic primary for D.C. mayor in a Washington Post-Schar School poll.

item.title: Man who plotted with au pair to kill his wife gets life sentence
item.description: Authorities had argued that Brendan Banfield, 41, murdered his wife and another man in the Banfields’ Northern Virginia home in a bid to be with the family’s au pair, who ultimately testified against him.

item.title: Why Trump suddenly softened his new green card policy
item.description: After President Donald Trump announced a green card policy last month requiring applicants to apply from abroad, big business, including tech and AI companies, launched a quiet but extensive lobbying effort against it.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: He cried, she laughed. Now her boyfriend has shut down completely.
item.description: Carolyn Hax: He cried, she laughed. Now her boyfriend has shut down completely.

item.title: As a doctor, I’d lost religion. Then came the electric scrubber.
item.description: Physicians are too quick to discount religious experience.

item.title: Trump officials planned to mark 2.7 million living people as dead, whistleblower claims
item.description: A former Social Security executive said the plan, which was not carried out, would have used a death database to pressure immigrants to leave the country.

item.title: Wager your points and finish off the week with today’s On the Record
item.description: Wager your points and finish off the week with today’s On the Record

item.title: U.S. employers added 172,000 jobs in May, a surprisingly strong showing
item.description: The unemployment rate held at 4.3 percent. Though the labor market has been growing at a stable but modest pace in 2026, job gains in May were greater than expected.

item.title: Payout fund. ICE deaths. Kennedy Center. DOGE plan. “Woke quotes.” Flesh-eating screwworms. Body image. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Senate votes to fund immigration agencies for the rest of Trump’s term, without limits on settlement fund
item.description: Senators rejected amendments meant to bar President Donald Trump from creating a fund that some feared could be used to compensate people who attacked the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021.

item.title: House passes bill with more than $8 billion in security assistance to Ukraine over objections of GOP leaders
item.description: The legislation reached the floor after six Republicans broke ranks to force a vote on the measure — the most robust aid package to advance in Congress in more than a year. The vote marked a show of bipartisan support for Ukraine in the fifth year of Russia’s full-scale invasion, with 18 Republicans joining Democrats in support.

item.title: House passes bill cutting food aid for pregnant women, children
item.description: Under the legislation, the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants and Children — more commonly known as WIC — would lose $141 million in funding for fruit and vegetable benefits for the nearly 5.4 million children and pregnant and postpartum women enrolled, according to an estimate from the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities. The Senate has yet to consider the legislation.

item.title: The science behind the precisely engineered grass on the World Cup's 16 fields
item.description: The World Cup starts June 11, but preparations for the fields started years ago.

item.title: ICE to stop reporting deaths of newly released detainees, internal memo says
item.description: The agency is facing pressure to improve medical care in its facilities after reporting the deaths of 18 detainees in the first five months of this year.
